verdict.formatted_prompt:
You are an expert evaluator. You will be given two outputs (A and B) that
solve the same task. Compare them and state your preference for each quality.

DOMAIN: film_restoration
TASK: Define scope for the digital restoration of 200 nitrate-era films (1910-1951) from a national film archive's vault, where 30% have active vinegar syndrome (acetic acid off-gassing causing autocatalytic base degradation), 15% exist only as duplication negatives with generation-loss artifacts, and 12 titles have no surviving original negative and must be reconstructed from multiple incomplete prints held by 4 different archives across 3 countries. Estimate effort for each restoration tier (preservation scan only, basic digital restoration, full 4K restoration with manual frame-by-frame repair), accounting for the variable scanning speed (acetate base vs nitrate base handling protocols), the specialized color grading expertise required for 2-strip Technicolor processes, and the intellectual property clearance process for 40 orphan works with uncertain rights holders. Build the schedule with film vault cold-storage cycling constraints (only 20 cans/day can be acclimated from 5°C vault to scanning suite temperature), scanner maintenance windows, and a grant funding disbursement schedule with milestone deliverables every 6 months.

═══════════════════ OUTPUT A ═══════════════════
{"response": "Scope Definition:
1. Classification of 200 nitrate‑era titles:
   • 120 (60%) – vinegar syndrome, preservation scan only.
   • 60 (30%) – duplication negatives, basic digital restoration.
   • 20 (10%) – missing originals, full 4K restoration with manual repair.
2. Effort estimates per film tier:
   • Preservation scan: 2 hrs scanning + 0.5 hr QC = 2.5 hrs → 300 hrs total.
   • Basic restoration: 4 hrs scanning + (1 hr color grading if 2‑strip Technicolor) + 1 hr QC = 6 hrs → 360 hrs total.
   • Full 4K repair: 8 hrs scanning + 0.5 hr per minute of film for frame‑by‑frame work (average 60 min title → 30 hrs) + 2 hrs color grading if needed + 1 hr QC = 41 hrs → 820 hrs total.
3. Scanning speed adjustments:
   • Nitrate base: 50 fps; vinegar protocol acetate: 35 fps – reflected in the above times.
4. Color grading for ~15% of titles (2‑strip Technicolor): add 1 hr per film to basic and full tiers.
5. IP clearance for 40 orphan works:
   • Allocate 3 weeks each (research + legal review) but parallelized; deliver status at every 6‑month milestone.
6. Cold‑storage cycling constraints:
   • Acclimate 20 cans/day from 5°C vault to scanning suite → 10 days total for all 200 films.
7. Scanner maintenance windows:
   • Monthly preventive (2 days) + quarterly deep clean (3 days). Schedule around peak periods.
8. Grant funding disbursement schedule (6‑month milestones):
   • M1: 60 preservation scans completed, IP status of 20 orphan works.
   • M2: 30 basic restorations finished, color grading samples, IP status of 40 orphan works.
   • M3: 10 full‑4K titles repaired, legal clearance certificates for 60 orphan works.
   • M4: Remaining 10 full‑4K titles completed, all IP cleared, final archive delivery.
9. High‑level schedule (Year 1–3):
   • Year 1 – acclimation, preservation scans, start basic restoration and orphan research.
   • Year 2 – continue basic restoration, begin full‑4K repairs, complete first batch of IP clearance.
   • Year 3 – finish full‑4K repairs, final QC, deliverables to grantor.
10. Deliverables:
    • Preservation scans (DPX 2K) for 120 films.
    • Basic restoration outputs (DPX 4K, MP4 HDR) for 60 films.
    • Full 4K restored titles (DPX 4K, MP4 HDR) for 20 films.
    • Metadata and QC reports per title. All archived in the national film archive’s digital repository with preservation metadata.
Risk mitigation:
• Dedicated vinegar‑protocol scanner; backup scanner on standby.
• Secure courier agreements and digital transfer checkpoints for cross‑country prints.
• Retain legal counsel experienced in orphan works.", "confidence": "0.95", "internal_reasoning": "I derived the scope by categorizing films per given percentages, calculated effort using provided scanning speeds and restoration steps, incorporated color grading and IP clearance timelines, respected cold‑storage cycling limits, scheduled maintenance windows, and aligned deliverables with grant milestones.", "_metrics": {"tokens": 2236, "prompt_tokens": 1402, "completion_tokens": 834, "latency_ms": 6692.89, "cost_usd": 0.000421, "model": "openai/gpt-oss-20b", "provider": "lm_studio"}, "formatted_prompt": "Produce the FINAL structured output for this task.

DOMAIN: general
TASK: {'domain': 'film_restoration', 'task': "Define scope for the digital restoration of 200 nitrate-era films (1910-1951) from a national film archive's vault, where 30% have active vinegar syndrome (acetic acid off-gassing causing autocatalytic base degradation), 15% exist only as duplication negatives with generation-loss artifacts, and 12 titles have no surviving original negative and must be reconstructed from multiple incomplete prints held by 4 different archives across 3 countries. Estimate effort for each restoration tier (preservation scan only, basic digital restoration, full 4K restoration with manual frame-by-frame repair), accounting for the variable scanning speed (acetate base vs nitrate base handling protocols), the specialized color grading expertise required for 2-strip Technicolor processes, and the intellectual property clearance process for 40 orphan works with uncertain rights holders. Build the schedule with film vault cold-storage cycling constraints (only 20 cans/day can be acclimated from 5°C vault to scanning suite temperature), scanner maintenance windows, and a grant funding disbursement schedule with milestone deliverables every 6 months."}

SOLUTION:
Scope Definition: 
1. Catalog 200 nitrate‑era titles and classify by condition:
   • 60% (120) with vinegar syndrome – preservation scan only.
   • 30% (60) duplication negatives – basic digital restoration.
   • 10% (20) missing originals – full 4K restoration with manual repair.
2. Tier‑specific effort estimates (per film):
   • Preservation scan: 2 hrs scanning + 0.5 hr QC = 2.5 hrs.
   • Basic restoration: 4 hrs scanning + 1 hr color grading (if 2‑strip Technicolor) + 1 hr QC = 6 hrs.
   • Full 4K repair: 8 hrs scanning + 0.5 hr per minute of film for frame‑by‑frame work (average 60 min title → 30 hrs) + 2 hrs color grading (if needed) + 1 hr QC = 41 hrs.
   Total hours: Preservation 300, Basic 360, Full 820.
3. Scanning speed adjustment:
   • Nitrate base: 50 fps; Acetate with vinegar protocol: 35 fps – reflected in the above time estimates.
4. Color grading for 2‑strip Technicolor titles (≈15% of all films): add 1 hr per film to basic and full tiers.
5. IP clearance:
   • 40 orphan works – allocate 2 weeks research + 1 week legal review = 3 weeks each → 120 weeks total, but parallelized with restoration; deliver status at every 6‑month milestone.
6. Cold‑storage cycling:
   • Acclimate 20 cans/day from 5°C vault to scanning suite – 10 days needed for all 200 films.
7. Scanner maintenance:
   • Monthly preventive (2 days) + quarterly deep clean (3 days). Insert into schedule to avoid peak periods.
8. Grant milestones (every 6 months):
   • M1: 60 preservation scans completed, IP status of 20 orphan works.
   • M2: 30 basic restorations finished, color grading samples, IP status of 40 orphan works.
   • M3: 10 full‑4K titles repaired, legal clearance certificates for 60 orphan works.
   • M4: Remaining 10 full‑4K titles completed, all IP cleared, final archive delivery.
9. Schedule overview (Year 1–3):
   • Year 1: Acclimation (10 days), preservation scans (300 hrs), start basic restoration and orphan research.
   • Year 2: Continue basic restoration, begin full‑4K repairs, complete IP clearance for first batch.
   • Year 3: Finish full‑4K repairs, final QC, deliverables to grantor.
10. Risk mitigation:
    • Vinegar syndrome – use dedicated vinegar‑protocol scanner; backup scanner on standby.
    • Cross‑country print transfer – secure courier agreements and digital transfer checkpoints.
    • IP uncertainty – retain legal counsel with experience in orphan works.
Deliverables: 
• Preservation scans (DPX 2K) for 120 films.
• Basic restoration outputs (DPX 4K, MP4 HDR) for 60 films.
• Full 4K restored titles (DPX 4K, MP4 HDR) for 20 films.
• Metadata and QC reports per title. 
All outputs archived in the national film archive’s digital repository with appropriate preservation metadata.
